name: Roach motel
summary: Easy to sign up, deliberately hard to cancel. The UX of exit is intentionally more difficult than the UX of entry.

evidence.claim: Brignull's canonical dark-patterns taxonomy lists 'roach motel' as deliberate entry-exit asymmetry.

California SB-313 and other state click-to-cancel laws require that cancellation be available through the same channel as signup with no more steps. The federal FTC Click-to-Cancel Rule was vacated by the 8th Circuit on July 8, 2025.

body.description: The user can sign up for a subscription, membership, or service in a few clicks, but cancellation requires navigating to a hidden page, calling during business hours only, speaking to a retention agent, or sending certified mail. Asymmetry of effort between entry and exit is the signature (E1). Prohibited by several US state click-to-cancel laws including CA SB-313 (E2).

detectionHeuristics.description: Compare signup click depth (typically 2-3 clicks) to cancellation click depth (often 5+ if available online at all).

body.llmVerifyPrompt: You are evaluating a subscription service's cancellation flow. Given the signup flow's click-to-completion count (typically 2-3 clicks) and the cancellation flow's path, determine whether the cancellation flow is 'click-to-cancel' (matches signup channel) or exhibits the 'roach motel' pattern (requires phone call, in-person cancellation, certified mail, or significantly more steps). Return: {hasRoachMotel: boolean, signupSteps: number, cancelSteps: number or 'phone-only', jurisdictionViolated: string or null}.
body.remediation: 1. Surface the cancellation-flow asymmetry to the user. 2. If the user is in a jurisdiction with a click-to-cancel law, draft a complaint letter. 3. Walk the user through the cancellation steps in plain language.
